summary: Steve Yegge shares diary entries from his first weeks as a Google employee ('Noogler') in 2005, describing the culture shock of abundant free food, the quirky perks like ball pits and massage chairs, and the exodus of Microsoft employees to Google's Kirkland office. The post humorously documents mishaps like losing his badge in a ball pit and escaping a boring tech talk, while painting a picture of Google as an engineering paradise where former Microsoft employees are thrilled to 'actually be writing code' again. The underlying message is that Google had become the gravitational center of tech talent, with an 'event horizon' that inevitably pulls in anyone who investigates it seriously.
key_points: Google's recruiting power had reached the point where investigating why people choose Google inevitably leads to applying there yourself

The Kirkland office was populated largely by ex-Microsoft employees who shared similar stories: cool projects that never launched, teams that hated each other, and bureaucratic weight crushing progress
Former Microsoft employees universally expressed relief at Google, saying they were 'actually writing code' again
The free food culture was so overwhelming that Yegge nearly got sick on his first day, comparing it to Willy Wonka's factory
Despite the perks and humor, Yegge jokes that Google is fattening employees up like the witch in Hansel and Gretel
money_quotes: Google has a sort of event horizon, beyond which you're inevitably sucked in.
Anyone at another company who looks hard enough into why people choose Google will ultimately apply to Google, if they have the courage.
Project was cool, never launched it, everyone hates each other.
If you ask anyone: 'are you glad you came to Google?' They always brighten up instantly and say: 'oh, hell yeah! I'm actually writing code!'
Nobody just gives you a bunch of free food for as long as you want without killing you and eating you.
They didn't prepare me adequately for the experience of being surrounded by yummy free food. I had approximately the same reaction as the kids when they first got to Willy Wonka's.
I think I'm the only person who's necessitated consulting the rules to see whether it counts if you serve, then score on yourself without the ball touching the other player's men.
key_insight: Google's combination of engineering autonomy, abundant perks, and freedom from bureaucracy created a talent black hole that was systematically draining other tech companies of their best engineers.
